title: نظام الاتصالات
description: The Saudi Telecommunications Act, issued by Royal Decree M/12, establishes the regulatory framework for the telecommunications sector in Saudi Arabia. The Act governs the licensing of telecommunications services, management of the radio frequency spectrum and numbering resources, consumer protection in telecommunications, interconnection and network access, and the powers of the Communications, Space and Technology Commission (CST) as the national regulatory authority.

provisions.title: Article 1. Definitions and Objectives
provisions.content: The Telecommunications Act governs the telecommunications sector in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia. Key objectives include: (1) developing a modern, efficient and competitive telecommunications sector serving the Kingdom's economic and social objectives; (2) ensuring the availability of reliable and affordable telecommunications services throughout the Kingdom; (3) promoting competition in the telecommunications market; (4) protecting the interests of consumers; (5) managing and optimising the use of the radio frequency spectrum as a strategic national resource; (6) ensuring the security and resilience of telecommunications networks. Definitions: 'Telecommunications' means the transmission, emission or reception of signals, signs, writing, images, sounds or information of any nature by wire, radio, optical or other electromagnetic systems; 'Licensee' means a person or entity holding a licence issued by the Commission; 'Commission' means the Communications, Space and Technology Commission (CST); 'Universal Service' means a defined minimum set of telecommunications services of specified quality available to all end-users at an affordable price.

provisions.title: Article 2. Communications, Space and Technology Commission (CST)
provisions.content: The Communications, Space and Technology Commission (CST) shall be the national regulatory authority for telecommunications. CST shall: (1) issue, modify, suspend and revoke licences for telecommunications services; (2) manage and allocate the radio frequency spectrum and numbering resources; (3) establish and enforce quality of service standards; (4) protect the rights and interests of end-users; (5) promote competition and prevent anti-competitive practices in the telecommunications market; (6) resolve disputes between licensees and between licensees and end-users; (7) cooperate with telecommunications regulatory authorities in other states and with international telecommunications organisations, including the International Telecommunication Union (ITU); (8) advise the government on telecommunications policy; (9) supervise compliance with the provisions of this Act and its implementing regulations. CST shall operate as an independent regulatory authority with financial and administrative independence. CST decisions shall be subject to judicial review by the Board of Grievances.

provisions.title: Article 3. Licensing of Telecommunications Services
provisions.content: No person shall provide telecommunications services in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia without a licence issued by CST. Licences for telecommunications services shall be of the following types: (1) comprehensive licence — for providers of public telecommunications services, fixed-line voice, mobile, and internet services; (2) service licence — for providers of value-added services; (3) class licence — for providers of services that are subject to a general authorisation without individual licensing; (4) spectrum licence — for use of designated radio frequencies. The grant of a comprehensive or service licence requires: (1) a completed application in the form prescribed by CST; (2) technical and financial capability to provide the licensed service; (3) commitment to the network coverage and rollout obligations specified by CST; (4) payment of the prescribed licence fee. Licences shall be for a period of up to 20 years and may be renewed. CST shall publish all licence terms and conditions and shall process licence applications transparently and without discrimination.

provisions.title: Article 4. Consumer Protection
provisions.content: Licensees providing telecommunications services to end-users shall: (1) provide services at tariffs published on their website and approved by CST; (2) provide end-users with a written contract specifying the terms and conditions of the service before commencing provision; (3) not terminate or suspend service without giving at least 15 days' notice, except in cases of non-payment or abuse; (4) provide end-users with itemised billing upon request; (5) implement number portability allowing end-users to retain their phone numbers when changing provider; (6) provide uninterrupted access to emergency services free of charge; (7) provide access for persons with disabilities to telecommunications services on terms equivalent to those available to others; (8) implement dispute resolution mechanisms accessible to end-users. CST shall establish a complaints mechanism for end-users and shall publish statistics on complaints and their resolution. Fines for violations of consumer protection obligations shall not exceed SAR 10,000,000.

provisions.title: Article 5. Radio Frequency Spectrum Management
provisions.content: The radio frequency spectrum in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia is a national resource owned by the State. CST shall manage the radio frequency spectrum in accordance with: (1) the national frequency allocation table maintained by CST; (2) the Radio Regulations of the International Telecommunication Union; (3) bilateral and multilateral frequency coordination agreements. Rights of use for radio frequencies shall be granted through: (1) individual allocation for licensed bands; (2) light licensing for specific applications; (3) licence-exempt operation for certain low-power uses. Frequency licences shall specify: the frequency band; the geographic area of use; the maximum power and technical parameters; the duration of the licence; and the applicable fees. Unauthorised use of the radio frequency spectrum shall result in a fine not exceeding SAR 5,000,000 and confiscation of equipment. Spectrum fees shall be determined by CST on the basis of the value of the frequency resource, the volume of spectrum used, and the geographic area covered.

provisions.title: Article 6. Network Security and Lawful Interception
provisions.content: Licensees shall: (1) implement appropriate technical and operational measures to ensure the security and resilience of their networks and services, including protection against cyberattacks, unauthorised access and service disruptions; (2) implement lawful interception capabilities in accordance with the technical specifications and requirements established by the competent security authorities; (3) report significant security incidents to CST and to competent security authorities within 24 hours; (4) implement business continuity plans ensuring service resilience during emergencies and disasters; (5) not use equipment from suppliers that have been prohibited by the competent security authorities on grounds of national security; (6) maintain technical logs and records required by competent authorities for a period of at least one year. In cases of national emergency or a threat to national security, the competent authority may require a licensee to suspend, restrict or operate its services in a specified manner. Licensees shall not disclose the existence of lawful interception orders to persons being monitored.

provisions.title: Article 7. Interconnection and Infrastructure Sharing
provisions.content: Licensees with significant market power (SMP) shall: (1) provide interconnection to their networks on transparent, reasonable and non-discriminatory terms; (2) publish a reference interconnection offer (RIO) approved by CST; (3) not apply terms and conditions more favourable to related parties than those offered to other licensees. All licensees shall share telecommunications infrastructure where technically feasible and economically reasonable, including masts, towers, ducts, conduits, and base stations. CST may impose infrastructure sharing obligations on SMP operators and may set the terms for sharing, including reference pricing. Disputes between licensees on interconnection or infrastructure sharing shall be resolved by CST within 90 days of referral, without prejudice to the right of the parties to seek judicial review. Licensees shall establish service level agreements for interconnection and share performance data with CST.

provisions.title: Article 8. Sanctions
provisions.content: Violations of this Act and its implementing regulations shall be subject to the following sanctions by CST: (1) warning — issued for minor or first-time violations with a requirement to remedy within a specified period; (2) financial penalties — not exceeding SAR 50,000,000 (fifty million Saudi Riyals) for serious violations, including provision of services without a licence; (3) suspension of licence — for repeated or serious violations; (4) revocation of licence — for persistent or particularly grave violations. Violations of national security-related obligations shall be referred to the competent security authority. Persons providing telecommunications services without a licence shall be additionally liable to imprisonment for up to two years. CST shall publish a register of violations and penalties imposed, which shall be updated quarterly. Decisions of CST imposing penalties may be appealed before the Board of Grievances within 60 days of notification.

definitions.definition: The transmission, emission or reception of signals, signs, writing, images, sounds or information of any nature by wire, radio, optical or other electromagnetic systems.
The Communications, Space and Technology Commission, the national regulatory authority for telecommunications in Saudi Arabia.
A person or entity holding a licence issued by the Commission (CST) to provide telecommunications services.
